data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: MXL, INTC, COUR, CHTR, MXL, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were mixed in the premarket  as investors weighed a complex geopolitical landscape against a backdrop of persistent energy risks.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • MaxLinear (MXL) +66% - Shares surged after delivering a strong Q1 beat and issuing well-above-consensus guidance, driven by a sharp inflection in its AI-linked optical data center business. The company guided Q2 revenue to $160M–$170M (vs. ~$144M estimates) and raised its 2026 optical data center outlook to $150M–$170M, citing accelerating demand for its Keystone interconnect products across hyperscale customers supporting 400G/800G AI workloads. While supply constraints and higher input costs persist, the upbeat outlook and visibility into AI infrastructure demand fueled the rally.
  • Intel (INTC) +25% - Shares jumped after delivering a strong Q1 beat and upbeat guidance, with adjusted EPS of $0.29 and revenue of $13.58B, both comfortably above expectations. Growth was driven by strength in Client Computing ($7.73B) and Data Center & AI ($5.05B, +22% Y/Y), while the company also pointed to sustained momentum with a sixth straight revenue beat. Looking ahead, Intel guided Q2 revenue to $13.8B–$14.8B and EPS to $0.20, both well ahead of consensus, as management highlighted rising demand from the shift toward AI inference and edge computing.
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +13% - Shares soared following stronger-than-expected results from Intel (INTC) and after D.A. Davidson & Co. upgraded the stock to Buy and sharply raised its price target, citing a “structural increase” in CPU demand driven by agentic AI. The call was reinforced by strong results from Intel Corporation, which analysts view as a positive read-through for AMD’s upcoming earnings, with expectations for upside revisions. The firm highlighted that rising demand for server CPUs in AI workloads could create supply tightness, positioning AMD to raise prices and expand margins, while benefiting from the broader data center buildout.
  • SAP SE (SAP) +7% - Shares gained after reporting Q1 results highlighted by strong cloud momentum, with cloud revenue jumping 27% (constant currency) and backlog surging 25% to $25.6B. While total revenue of $11.17B came in line with estimates, robust cloud growth and visibility supported outlook confidence, with the company guiding FY cloud revenue to $30.1B–$30.6B and combined cloud/software revenue growth of 12%–13%.
  • Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) +4% - Shares rose after JPMorgan Chase & Co. initiated coverage with an Overweight rating, citing improving fundamentals and multiple growth catalysts. The firm highlighted a stabilizing GLP-1 business, the recent partnership with Novo Nordisk reducing legal overhang, and potential upside from peptide legalization, where HIMS could benefit as an early mover. JPMorgan also sees revenue reacceleration in the second half and further branded partnerships supporting growth, setting a $35 price target (~24% upside).

Biggest stock losers

  • Coursera (COUR) -15% - Shares fell sharply after a wider-than-expected GAAP loss and declining free cash flow overshadowed otherwise solid operating metrics, including double-digit consumer revenue growth and a record 7.6M new learners in Q1. The company posted a net loss of $20.5M, nearly triple Y/Y, while maintaining full-year revenue guidance of $805M–$815M (in line with estimates) and projecting Q2 revenue slightly below consensus. Despite strong user growth and a stable long-term outlook, profitability concerns and softer near-term guidance weighed on sentiment.
  • Charter Communications (CHTR) -15% - Shares declined after mixed Q1 results, as a larger-than-expected broadband subscriber loss overshadowed modest strength elsewhere. The company lost 120K internet customers (vs. -100K expected), while mobile additions and better-than-feared video losses failed to offset weakness across core services. Revenue declined 1.1% to $13.5B (miss), EPS of $9.17 also missed, and EBITDA fell 2.2%, with free cash flow down 12.5%, reinforcing concerns around competitive pressure and slowing growth despite management’s longer-term improvement outlook.
  • HCA Healthcare (HCA) -7% - Shares tumbled after Q1 results showed softer-than-expected profitability and a lack of typical seasonal volume uplift, despite a modest revenue beat. While revenue rose ~4% Y/Y to $19.1B, adjusted EBITDA missed estimates, and margins compressed ~50 bps, reflecting weaker patient volumes driven by a sharp drop in respiratory cases and weather disruptions. The company also reiterated full-year guidance that remains below consensus, reinforcing concerns around volume trends and near-term earnings momentum.
  • Boyd Gaming (BYD) -6% - Shares slid after the company reported Q1 results that missed expectations, with non-GAAP EPS of $1.60 (-$0.11 miss) and revenue of $997.4M (+0.6% Y/Y), slightly below estimates. While management reiterated full-year expectations for its Online ($30M–$35M EBITDAR) and Managed & Other ($110M–$114M EBITDAR) segments, investor focus remained on ongoing pressure in the Las Vegas Locals segment, where destination trends and Suncoast-related disruptions have been weighing on performance. CFO commentary suggested a ~$5M–$6M quarterly EBITDAR drag persisting into Q2, with pressures expected through Q3 before gradual improvement, though a full recovery and return to growth may not materialize until early next year.
  • Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) -3% - Shares dipped after data showed its weight-loss drugs lagging rival uptake, with prescriptions for Zepbound declining ~2% week-over-week while Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy grew 7%. Early launch data for Lilly’s oral therapy Foundayo also came in soft, with ~3.7K scripts in its first full week versus ~18.4K for Novo’s comparable launch. While analysts cautioned against overinterpreting early data, the slower prescription trends weighed on sentiment as investors reassessed Lilly’s positioning in the obesity market.

data.title: Intel clears dot-com era peak in major technical breakout post blowout earnings
data.description:
Intel (INTC) shares soared more than 25% on Friday, eclipsing their dot-com era record high after the chipmaker reported first-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street expectations.

Shares were recently trading near $83.87 at market open, up 25.6%, far above the stock’s 50-day moving average of $50.35, 100-day moving average of $46.65, and 200-day moving average of $38.15, signaling a sharp breakout in momentum.

The stock’s RSI of 70 suggests shares are entering overbought territory after the surge, which could lead to near-term volatility even as momentum remains strong.

With the stock now in price discovery mode at fresh highs, traders are likely watching whether Intel can hold above the $80 level during the regular session. Sustained trading above that mark could reinforce bullish sentiment, while any pullback may see initial support emerge near the prior close of $66.78.

Intel (INTC) options were pricing a nearly 10% post-earnings move ahead of the chipmaker’s first-quarter results.

For the quarter ended March 28, Intel reported adjusted earnings of $0.29 per share on revenue of $13.58B, up 7.2% year over year. Looking ahead, the firm expects second-quarter revenue to be between $13.8 and $14.8B, with the midpoint of $14.3B well above the $13.04B analysts were anticipating. It also anticipates earning an adjusted $0.20 per share, compared to the estimates of $0.09 per share.

data.title: Metaplanet issues ¥8B bonds for BTC; zero-yield hits shares
data.description:
Metaplanet (MTPLF) announced on Friday that it will issue its 20th series of ordinary bonds worth ¥8B to EVO FUND. The main purpose of raising this fund is to buy more Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Each bond has a face value of ¥200.00M and carries zero interest, meaning investors won’t earn periodic returns. The bonds will be issued on April 24, 2026, and will be fully repaid at face value on April 23, 2027, according to the official filing.

The company can also repay the bonds early if the bondholder gives a 5-day notice or under certain future financing conditions. These bonds are unsecured, meaning there is no collateral or guarantee. Moreover, the full bond issue is allocated only to EVO FUND.

Currently, Metaplanet (MTPLF) holds about ~40.18K BTC, which is roughly ~0.91% of Bitcoin’s total supply. This makes it the third-largest corporate Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holder, after MicroStrategy (MSTR) and Twenty One Capital (XXI).

After the announcement, MTPLF stock fell to ~¥339, down around ~4% from the previous close of ~¥352. The company expects this bond issuance to have a mild impact on its FY26 financial results.

data.title: S&P 500 workforce shrinks in 2025 for first time since 2016
data.description:
Employment across S&P 500 (SP500) companies declined in 2025, marking the first annual drop in nearly a decade, according to Bank of America Global Research, with data compiled alongside Bloomberg.

Total headcount slipped to about 28.1M, ending a multi-year expansion that had steadily lifted corporate payrolls since the pandemic recovery.

The reversal suggests a shift in corporate strategy as firms prioritize efficiency, automation, and margin protection amid a slowing macro backdrop. Layoffs have been concentrated in sectors such as technology and financials, where cost discipline and restructuring efforts have intensified.

While the decline is modest relative to the broader labor market, it underscores a cooling in hiring appetite among large-cap companies that have historically driven employment growth.
